verses.text: In the twenty and seventh year of Jeroboam king of Israel began Azariah son of Amaziah king of Judah to reign. Sixteen years old was he when he began to reign, and he reigned two and fifty years in Jerusalem. And his mother's name was Jecholiah of Jerusalem. And he did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, according to all that his father Amaziah had done; Save that the high places were not removed: the people sacrificed and burnt incense still on the high places. Guidance: Azariah (Uzziah) — a long and largely faithful reign. Azariah (called Uzziah in Isaiah, Amos, Hosea, and Chronicles) reigns 52 years — the longest reign in Judah's history. His mother was Jecholiah of Jerusalem. The verdict is favorable: 'he did that which was right in the sight of the LORD, according to all that his father Amaziah had done' — with the recurring qualifier: 'save that the high places were not removed.' The chapter is showing us a king of genuine, sustained faithfulness — 2 Chronicles 26 records his great achievements (military victories, fortifications, agriculture, a strong army) and notes 'he sought God in the days of Zechariah... and as long as he sought the LORD, God made him to prosper.' His reign was Judah's golden age, parallel to Jeroboam II's in the north, and the era when Isaiah, Amos, and Hosea began to prophesy ('In the year that king Uzziah died I saw also the Lord' — Isa 6:1). The chapter is teaching us, by his long and prosperous reign, that sustained seeking of the LORD brings stability and blessing. Yet the persistent flaw — the unremoved high places — shows even this good king's reform was incomplete, and 2 Chronicles records his tragic late pride (the unlawful incense-offering) that brought the leprosy mentioned in the next verse. The chapter is teaching us that a long life of doing right can still carry an unaddressed compromise and end with a sobering chastening — faithfulness must be maintained to the end, and even a great king is not above the LORD's boundaries.

And the LORD smote the king, so that he was a leper unto the day of his death, and dwelt in a several house. And Jotham the king's son was over the house, judging the people of the land. And the rest of the acts of Azariah, and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah? So Azariah slept with his fathers; and they buried him with his fathers in the city of David: and Jotham his son reigned in his stead. Guidance: The leprosy of a great king. 'The LORD smote the king, so that he was a leper unto the day of his death, and dwelt in a several house' (a separate house — isolated, as the law required of lepers, Lev 13:46). Kings of Kings states the affliction plainly; 2 Chronicles 26:16-21 gives the cause: at the height of his strength 'his heart was lifted up to his destruction,' and he entered the temple to burn incense — a priestly act forbidden to kings — and when 80 priests resisted him, the leprosy broke out on his forehead even as he raged. His son Jotham administered the kingdom during his father's quarantined final years. The chapter is showing us the tragic late chapter of a great reign. Uzziah's very success became the soil of a pride that overstepped the LORD's appointed boundaries between king and priest. The chapter is teaching us a sobering lesson that echoes Jehu, Amaziah, and now Uzziah: prosperity and power are spiritually dangerous, and a heart 'lifted up' is the recurring downfall of strong men. Uzziah was not destroyed for the high places he tolerated but for the holy boundary he transgressed in his pride. The chapter is also teaching us that the LORD guards the distinction between offices He has ordained — the king may not seize the priest's role; each must keep to his calling. Even a 52-year faithful king, in one act of proud presumption, was struck and spent his last years isolated, the disease a visible mark of an invisible overreach. The chapter is teaching us to finish well: greatness sustained for decades can still be marred at the end by the pride that success breeds, and reverence for God's holy limits must outlast every achievement.

In the thirty and eighth year of Azariah king of Judah did Zachariah the son of Jeroboam reign over Israel in Samaria six months. And he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, as his fathers had done: he departed not from the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, who made Israel to sin. And Shallum the son of Jabesh conspired against him, and smote him before the people, and slew him, and reigned in his stead. And the rest of the acts of Zachariah, behold, they are written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel. This was the word of the LORD which he spake unto Jehu, saying, Thy sons shall sit on the throne of Israel unto the fourth generation. And so it came to pass. Guidance: The fourth generation ends — and the north's collapse begins. Zechariah, son of Jeroboam II, reigns a mere six months. He is evil like his fathers; he keeps the calf-worship. Then Shallum conspires, strikes him down 'before the people' (a public, brazen assassination), and seizes the throne. The narrator immediately draws the theological line: 'This was the word of the LORD which he spake unto Jehu, saying, Thy sons shall sit on the throne of Israel unto the fourth generation. And so it came to pass.' Zechariah was the fourth generation (Jehu → Jehoahaz → Jehoash → Jeroboam II → Zechariah); with his murder the Jehu dynasty ends exactly as the LORD had said in 10:30 — four generations, no more. The chapter is showing us the LORD's word fulfilled with precise dual force: the promise of four generations was kept (a real reward for Jehu's service against Ahab), AND its limit was kept (the half-hearted obedience earned no fifth generation). The chapter is teaching us that God's promises are exact in both directions — He gives all He pledges and not a generation beyond. The chapter is also marking a grim turning point: with the Jehu dynasty gone, the northern kingdom enters its death-spiral. From here to the exile (ch 17), Israel will see a rapid succession of usurpers and assassinations — Shallum, Menahem, Pekahiah, Pekah, Hoshea — most taking the throne by murder. The stable century of Jehu's house is over; the chapter is teaching us that when a kingdom persists in foundational sin, the removal of even an imperfect stability ushers in chaos. The clock the LORD set has struck, and Israel's final unraveling has begun.

Shallum the son of Jabesh began to reign in the nine and thirtieth year of Uzziah king of Judah; and he reigned a full month in Samaria. For Menahem the son of Gadi went up from Tirzah, and came to Samaria, and smote Shallum the son of Jabesh in Samaria, and slew him, and reigned in his stead. And the rest of the acts of Shallum, and his conspiracy which he made, behold, they are written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel. Then Menahem smote Tiphsah, and all that were therein, and the coasts thereof from Tirzah: because they opened not to him, therefore he smote it; and all the women therein that were with child he ripped up. Guidance: A one-month king and a brutal usurper. Shallum's reign lasts 'a full month' — he murdered his way to the throne and held it just thirty days before Menahem came up from Tirzah (the old northern capital), killed him in Samaria, and seized power in turn. The assassin is assassinated; the usurper is usurped. Then the chapter records Menahem's first act of consolidation with horrifying brutality: when the city of Tiphsah refused to open its gates to him, he smote it and 'all the women therein that were with child he ripped up' — the very atrocity Elisha had foreseen Hazael committing against Israel (8:12), now committed by an Israelite king against his own people. The chapter is showing us the moral collapse of the northern kingdom in fast-forward: kings rising and falling by murder within weeks, a ruler butchering pregnant women in his own land to terrorize a town into submission. The chapter is teaching us what happens when a nation abandons the LORD and the restraining structures fail — power becomes pure violence, and the horrors a prophet once wept to imagine an enemy doing are now done by the nation's own rulers. The chapter is also showing us the inversion that sin produces: Israel, called to be the LORD's people, now matches and even initiates the cruelty of the pagan nations around it. The chapter is teaching us that there is no floor to how far a people can fall once the fear of God is gone — the kingdom is not merely weak now but savage, and its own kings have become its worst affliction.

For Menahem the son of Gadi went up from Tirzah, and came to Samaria, and smote Shallum the son of Jabesh in Samaria, and slew him, and reigned in his stead. And the rest of the acts of Shallum, and his conspiracy which he made, behold, they are written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel. Then Menahem smote Tiphsah, and all that were therein, and the coasts thereof from Tirzah: because they opened not to him, therefore he smote it; and all the women therein that were with child he ripped up. Guidance: A one-month king and a brutal usurper. Shallum's reign lasts 'a full month' — he murdered his way to the throne and held it just thirty days before Menahem came up from Tirzah (the old northern capital), killed him in Samaria, and seized power in turn. The assassin is assassinated; the usurper is usurped. Then the chapter records Menahem's first act of consolidation with horrifying brutality: when the city of Tiphsah refused to open its gates to him, he smote it and 'all the women therein that were with child he ripped up' — the very atrocity Elisha had foreseen Hazael committing against Israel (8:12), now committed by an Israelite king against his own people. The chapter is showing us the moral collapse of the northern kingdom in fast-forward: kings rising and falling by murder within weeks, a ruler butchering pregnant women in his own land to terrorize a town into submission. The chapter is teaching us what happens when a nation abandons the LORD and the restraining structures fail — power becomes pure violence, and the horrors a prophet once wept to imagine an enemy doing are now done by the nation's own rulers. The chapter is also showing us the inversion that sin produces: Israel, called to be the LORD's people, now matches and even initiates the cruelty of the pagan nations around it. The chapter is teaching us that there is no floor to how far a people can fall once the fear of God is gone — the kingdom is not merely weak now but savage, and its own kings have become its worst affliction.

In the nine and thirtieth year of Azariah king of Judah began Menahem the son of Gadi to reign over Israel, and reigned ten years in Samaria. And he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD: he departed not all his days from the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, who made Israel to sin. And Pul the king of Assyria came against the land: and Menahem gave Pul a thousand talents of silver, that his hand might be with him to confirm the kingdom in his hand. And Menahem exacted the money of Israel, even of all the mighty men of wealth, of each man fifty shekels of silver, to give to the king of Assyria. So the king of Assyria turned back, and stayed not there in the land. Guidance: Assyria appears — and Israel begins to buy survival. Menahem reigns ten years, evil like all the rest. The momentous new development: 'Pul the king of Assyria came against the land' — the first appearance in Kings of the empire that will eventually destroy the northern kingdom. (Pul is Tiglath-pileser III, the great Assyrian conqueror whose campaigns reshaped the ancient Near East.) Menahem buys him off with a thousand talents of silver — an enormous sum — 'that his hand might be with him to confirm the kingdom in his hand.' Note the motive: Menahem pays not only to make Assyria withdraw but to secure Assyrian backing for his own shaky, usurped throne. He raises the money by taxing the wealthy fifty shekels of silver per head. Assyria takes the tribute and withdraws — for now. The chapter is showing us the beginning of the end. The shadow of Assyria has fallen over Israel, and the northern kingdom's response is not repentance but appeasement — buying time and propping up an illegitimate ruler with silver squeezed from the rich. The chapter is teaching us that nations (and people) in decline often respond to looming judgment by managing symptoms rather than addressing the cause: Menahem pays the immediate threat to go away and never considers that the real problem is Israel's sin, which has invited the LORD's discipline. The chapter is also showing us the seed of dependence: by inviting Assyrian 'help' to confirm his throne, Menahem draws Israel into the orbit of the very empire that will swallow it. The chapter is teaching us that bribing a predator to protect you only feeds the predator and confirms its claim on you — Israel's silver buys a reprieve that hastens the eventual ruin.

And the rest of the acts of Menahem, and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel? And Menahem slept with his fathers; and Pekahiah his son reigned in his stead. In the fiftieth year of Azariah king of Judah Pekahiah the son of Menahem began to reign over Israel in Samaria, and reigned two years. And he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD: he departed not from the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, who made Israel to sin. But Pekah the son of Remaliah, a captain of his, conspired against him, and smote him in Samaria, in the palace of the king's house, with Argob and Arieh, and with him fifty men of the Gileadites: and he killed him, and reigned in his room. Guidance: Pekahiah's brief reign and another assassination. Menahem dies (one of the few northern kings of this era to die naturally and pass the throne to his son), and Pekahiah reigns two years — evil, keeping Jeroboam's sins. Then his own military officer, Pekah son of Remaliah, conspires with fifty Gileadites and two named accomplices (Argob and Arieh), kills Pekahiah in the palace fortress at Samaria, and takes the throne. The chapter is showing us the relentless continuation of the pattern: another king, another two-year reign, another assassination by a trusted captain. The detail that the killer was 'a captain of his' — an officer Pekahiah trusted — and used Gileadite troops shows the throne is now held only by whoever commands the most loyal swords, and even those swords turn. The chapter is teaching us the political fruit of a kingdom that has cut itself off from the LORD: there is no legitimacy left, only force, and force is forever vulnerable to greater or more sudden force. Each usurper rules in fear of the next. The chapter is also quietly advancing the countdown to exile — Pekah, who now seizes power, will be the king under whom Assyria first deports large portions of Israel (next verses). The chapter is teaching us that a society which has abandoned its covenant with God loses the very basis of stable, legitimate authority; what remains is a grim cycle of murder where each ruler is both assassin and eventual victim.

In the two and fiftieth year of Azariah king of Judah Pekah the son of Remaliah began to reign over Israel in Samaria, and reigned twenty years. And he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD: he departed not from the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, who made Israel to sin. In the days of Pekah king of Israel came Tiglathpileser king of Assyria, and took Ijon, and Abelbethmaachah, and Janoah, and Kedesh, and Hazor, and Gilead, and Galilee, all the land of Naphtali, and carried them captive to Assyria. And Hoshea the son of Elah made a conspiracy against Pekah the son of Remaliah, and smote him, and slew him, and reigned in his stead, in the twentieth year of Jotham the son of Uzziah. Guidance: The first great deportation. Pekah reigns 20 years, evil. In his days the blow long threatened finally falls: 'came Tiglath-pileser king of Assyria, and took' a string of northern cities — Ijon, Abel-beth-maachah, Janoah, Kedesh, Hazor — and whole regions: Gilead (the Transjordan), Galilee, 'all the land of Naphtali.' And then the dreaded word: 'and carried them captive to Assyria.' This is the first mass deportation of Israelites — the Assyrian policy of uprooting conquered populations and resettling them far away to break national identity. The northern and eastern thirds of Israel are stripped away and their people exiled, a generation before Samaria itself falls (ch 17). Then Pekah too is assassinated — Hoshea son of Elah conspires, kills him, and takes the throne (the last northern king before the final fall). The chapter is showing us the covenant curse of exile beginning to be executed (Deut 28:36, 64). The LORD's patience, extended through Jeroboam II's reprieve (14:26-27), is now running out; the discipline escalates from oppression to deportation. The chapter is teaching us that there is an end to the time God grants for repentance — Israel had centuries of prophets, warnings, oppressions, and mercies, and persisted in the calf-worship; now the exile that the prophets foretold has begun in earnest. The chapter is also showing us the relentless logic of the north's collapse: even as the kingdom loses half its territory to Assyria, its internal politics continue the murder-cycle (Pekah killed by Hoshea). A people facing national extinction still cannot stop killing its own kings. The chapter is teaching us the terrible momentum of judgment unheeded: the exile has started, the kingdom is dismembered, and Samaria's final hour is now only one reign away.

In the two and fiftieth year of Azariah king of Judah Pekah the son of Remaliah began to reign over Israel in Samaria, and reigned twenty years. And he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD: he departed not from the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, who made Israel to sin. In the days of Pekah king of Israel came Tiglathpileser king of Assyria, and took Ijon, and Abelbethmaachah, and Janoah, and Kedesh, and Hazor, and Gilead, and Galilee, all the land of Naphtali, and carried them captive to Assyria. And Hoshea the son of Elah made a conspiracy against Pekah the son of Remaliah, and smote him, and slew him, and reigned in his stead, in the twentieth year of Jotham the son of Uzziah. Guidance: The first great deportation. Pekah reigns 20 years, evil. In his days the blow long threatened finally falls: 'came Tiglath-pileser king of Assyria, and took' a string of northern cities — Ijon, Abel-beth-maachah, Janoah, Kedesh, Hazor — and whole regions: Gilead (the Transjordan), Galilee, 'all the land of Naphtali.' And then the dreaded word: 'and carried them captive to Assyria.' This is the first mass deportation of Israelites — the Assyrian policy of uprooting conquered populations and resettling them far away to break national identity. The northern and eastern thirds of Israel are stripped away and their people exiled, a generation before Samaria itself falls (ch 17). Then Pekah too is assassinated — Hoshea son of Elah conspires, kills him, and takes the throne (the last northern king before the final fall). The chapter is showing us the covenant curse of exile beginning to be executed (Deut 28:36, 64). The LORD's patience, extended through Jeroboam II's reprieve (14:26-27), is now running out; the discipline escalates from oppression to deportation. The chapter is teaching us that there is an end to the time God grants for repentance — Israel had centuries of prophets, warnings, oppressions, and mercies, and persisted in the calf-worship; now the exile that the prophets foretold has begun in earnest. The chapter is also showing us the relentless logic of the north's collapse: even as the kingdom loses half its territory to Assyria, its internal politics continue the murder-cycle (Pekah killed by Hoshea). A people facing national extinction still cannot stop killing its own kings. The chapter is teaching us the terrible momentum of judgment unheeded: the exile has started, the kingdom is dismembered, and Samaria's final hour is now only one reign away.

In the second year of Pekah the son of Remaliah king of Israel began Jotham the son of Uzziah king of Judah to reign. Five and twenty years old was he when he began to reign, and he reigned sixteen years in Jerusalem. And his mother's name was Jerusha, the daughter of Zadok. And he did that which was right in the sight of the LORD: he did according to all that his father Uzziah had done. Howbeit the high places were not removed: the people sacrificed and burned incense still in the high places. He built the higher gate of the house of the LORD. Guidance: Jotham of Judah — quiet faithfulness amid gathering storm. Back in the south, Jotham (who had administered the kingdom during his father Uzziah's leprous final years) now reigns in his own right, 16 years. His mother was Jerusha daughter of Zadok. The verdict is favorable: 'he did that which was right in the sight of the LORD... according to all that his father Uzziah had done' — with the familiar qualifier about the high places. His one noted building project: 'He built the higher gate of the house of the LORD' — continuing the care for the temple that marks Judah's good kings. (2 Chr 27 adds that Jotham 'became mighty, because he prepared his ways before the LORD his God' and notably did NOT repeat his father's fatal temple-intrusion — he 'entered not into the temple of the LORD,' learning from Uzziah's pride.) The chapter is showing us a genuinely good king reigning steadily in Judah even as the northern kingdom disintegrates next door. The contrast is stark: while Israel cycles through assassinations and loses territory to Assyria, Judah under Jotham enjoys stability, faithfulness, and temple-building. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's covenant with David continues to bear fruit in the south — the Davidic line provides a continuity and faithfulness the breakaway north never had. The chapter is also teaching us a quiet lesson about learning from a father's failure: Jotham did right 'according to all that his father did' in the good things, but pointedly avoided the proud temple-intrusion that destroyed his father. He inherited the virtues and refused the fatal flaw. The chapter is teaching us that a son can honor a parent's faith while learning from the parent's sin — taking the good and leaving the error is the mark of wisdom.

Now the rest of the acts of Jotham, and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah? In those days the LORD began to send against Judah Rezin the king of Syria, and Pekah the son of Remaliah. And Jotham slept with his fathers, and was buried with his fathers in the city of David his father: and Ahaz his son reigned in his stead. Guidance: Jotham's close and the gathering threat against Judah. The reign closes with the standard formula, but with an ominous note attached: 'In those days the LORD began to send against Judah Rezin the king of Syria, and Pekah the son of Remaliah.' The Aramean-Israelite alliance (the 'Syro-Ephraimite' coalition) begins to pressure Judah — the threat that will dominate the next reign and provoke the great crisis of Isaiah 7. Note the theology: 'the LORD began to send' these enemies; even Judah's troubles are under His sovereign hand. Jotham dies in peace and is buried with his fathers; his son Ahaz succeeds. The chapter is closing on a hinge between eras. Jotham's reign was good and stable, but the storm clouds are gathering: the Syro-Ephraimite threat begins, and the successor, Ahaz, will prove to be one of Judah's most faithless kings (ch 16) — the one who will strip the temple to bribe Assyria and introduce pagan altars into Jerusalem. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD governs the rise of national threats as instruments of His purpose — Rezin and Pekah do not move except as He 'sends' them. The chapter is also teaching us the sober realism of generational decline: a good father (Jotham) is succeeded by a wicked son (Ahaz), just as in the north faithful periods gave way to apostasy. The covenant lamp still burns in David's line, but the next bearer will nearly extinguish his own devotion. The chapter ends the long survey of ch 15 — Judah's golden-age kings (Uzziah, Jotham) set beside the north's death-spiral — with the threat that will test whether Judah, too, will trust the LORD or trust in foreign powers. That test belongs to Ahaz, and to the prophet Isaiah who will confront him.
